## BY  MR. JOHNSON:

- Q. As I just  introduced  myself, I'm  Darrell Johnson. I'm  one  of  the  attorneys  with  the  United States. This  is  Pat  Bryan. He is  also  an attorney  from  the  United  States.

We have  different  roles  here  because  I work in  the  Defense  Section,  he  works  in  the Enforcement  Section,  so  what  that  means  is  I'm here  to  talk  to  you  about  a  very  narrow  issue  as it  relates  to  Otis  Air  Force  Base  and  Naval  Air Station  at  Quonset  Point  because  they've  been sued.

- A.
- Why  the  hell  are  they  suing  me?
- Q. Well, I'm  showing  you to  start  off with  I am  going  to  show  you  -­ I'm  not  going  to mark  this  as  an  exhibit,  but  it's  a Second  Amended Complaint. It's  Document  69  from  the  docket  of

## RAYMOND  NADEAU,

having  been  first  duly  sworn,  was  deposed  and testified  as  follows:

- Emhart  Industries  vs.  United  States  Air  Force, this  case,  et  al.,  and  essentially  what the reason  I'm here  and  what  I'd like  to  speak  to  you about  -­
- A. I can't read  it. I don't have  my glasses.

MR. JOHNSON: Okay. No  problem.

- Q. And  there  will  be  some  documents  as  we go  through. We did  bring  a  magnifying  glass,  if that  will  help. If  it doesn't,  I can  read  you  the portions.
- A. Okay.
- Q. But  essentially  the  United  States  Air Force  and  the  United  States  Navy  have  been  sued and  the  allegation  is  that  in  the  1960s  New England  Container  received  at  least  4800  drums from  Quonset  or  Otis  and  that's based  in  part  on what  -­ on  what  you've  said  in  prior  statements and  testimony.
- A. I don't remember  that.
- Q. And  Emhart  alleges  that  these  and  other drums  received  by  New  England  Container  from Quonset  and  Otis  contained  chemical  substances including  tactical  military  herbicides. So  I know you've -­

- Q. - A. Okay.
- Q. And  so  I'll work  hard  to  try  to  make sure  I let  you  finish  an  answer  before  I ask another  question.
- A. Okay.
- Q. And  I'd  ask  that  you  wait  until  I finish asking  a question  to  provide  an  answer, and  along those  lines,  the  answer  has  to  be  out  loud, so where  normally  you  could  shake  your  head  and  I would  understand  you  mean  yes, she  cannot  write that  down, so  if  you  can  say  it out  loud.

Then  also  if  you  speak  -even  if  somebody makes  an  objection  from  this  side  of  the  room, speak  to  me or  the  court  reporter  so  that  she  can hear  what  you  are  saying.

- A. Okay.
- Q. Let's  see, I'll try  to  go  over  all  the ground  rules  here. I know  you've  been  deposed before  but  we'll  go  over  it again. I'll try  and

This  was  before  the  United  States  became a party  to  the  case  but  it  is  actually  still  part of  this  case  and  it's  marked  Bates-stamp  Emhart 009087  and  it does  not  include  exhibits. It's  a mini  transcript  and  it ends  at  Emhart  009105, so this  one  was  more  recent,  about  five  years  ago. Do  you  remember  this  deposition?

## BY  MR. JOHNSON:

- Q. I'm  going  to  direct  you  on  Exhibit  1  to Page  64 of  the  exhibit. It's  Bates  ending  in 4450. And  there  you  testified  that  you  worked  at New  England  Container  -­ you  worked  at  New  England Container  Company  from  about  1956  till  1969?

- Q. Is  that  your  recollection  today?
- A. Yep.
- Q. And  that  during  the  last  seven  years that  you  were  with  New  England  Container  Company, that  you  were  a truck  driver?
- A. Right.
- Q. Okay. That's  still  your  testimony today?
- A. Yes.
- Q. Okay. Good. Now, there  you  discussed your  daily  routine  a little bit  and  your  testimony was  that  the  first  thing  you  do  coming  in  the morning  -this  is  while  you  were  a truck driver  -you'd  come  in  and  see  if  there  were  any deliveries,  load  the  truck  and  go  deliver  them?
- A. Yes.
- Q. Okay. So  you  agree  with  that  today  too?
- A. Yes.
- Q. So  what  I just  kind  of  want  to  go  from there  is  how  was  that  communicated  to  you? How did  you  know  if  there  were  deliveries?
- A. The  boss  would  tell  me. The  boss  would say, get  the  truck  and  bring  it up  to  the  door  and you're  going  to  go  to  such-and-such  a place.
